An analysis of the body’s development provides some insight into the issue.
Because the epiphyseal plates of the bones of prepubescent children have yet to close (33, 40), depth jumps and other high-intensity lower body drills are contraindicated (2, 32, 39).
While the growth plates are open, highly intense activity and injury may cause them to close prematurely, resulting in limb length discrepancies (32).

Further, and as with all forms of exercise, boys and girls should have the emotional maturity to accept and follow directions and should be able to appreciate the benefits and concerns associated with this method of training.
Empirically, 7- and 8-year-olds have been trained in progressive plyometric programs, and they continue to lead active lives as teenagers and adults (17).

Plyometric exercise programs for children should be used to develop the neuromuscular control and the anaerobic skills that will carry over to participation in sport and athletics, both during childhood and as they advance to higher levels of competition.
For these reasons, it is extremely important to gradually progress from relatively simple to more complex drills.
It is important to focus on the quality of the movements (e.g., proper body alignment and speed of movement) to develop techniques that will be essential for more advanced exercises.
As with adults, recovery between workouts must be adequate to prevent overtraining.

While the optimal amount of recovery needed between plyometric workouts is unknown, it should vary based on the intensity of the training program and the athlete’s skills, abilities, and tolerances as well as on the time of year (i.e., off-season, preseason, or in-season).
Therefore, a minimum of two to three days between plyometric workouts should be considered essential to optimize adaptations to the training program and minimize the athlete’s risk of injury (17).
Under proper supervision and with an appropriate program, prepubescent and adolescent children may perform plyometric exercises.
Depth jumps and high-intensity lower body plyometrics are contraindicated for this population.
Masters athletes are finding that they can maintain their physical capabilities late into life and are looking for additional training insights.
When designing a plyometric training program for a masters athlete, the strength and conditioning professional needs to be specific in deciding on the goal or goals of the program.
Some primary issues to consider are any preexisting orthopedic conditions (such as osteoarthritis or any sort of surgical joint intervention) or joint degeneration.
These call for even greater caution and a more careful use of plyometric exercise.

For example, a healthy masters athlete without surgical history who wants to improve his or her running performance should use depth jumps and single-leg exercises cautiously, so alternate-leg bounding and the double-leg hop would be better choices.

Similarly, a masters runner with a history of knee surgery such as partial meniscus removal, or with significant joint degeneration, should consider depth jumps and single-leg plyometric exercises contraindicated and should use other forms of plyometrics cautiously.

After consideration of the predispositions of the masters athlete’s physical condition, a plyometric program should be designed according to the same guidelines as outlined for adult athletes, with the following changes.

The plyometric program should include no more than five low- to moderate-intensity exercises; the volume should be lower, that is, should include fewer total foot contacts than a standard plyometric training program; and the recovery time between plyometric workouts should be three to
Plyometric exercise is only one part of an athlete’s overall training program.
Many sports and activities use multiple energy systems or require other forms of exercise to properly prepare athletes for competition.
Each energy system and sport-specific need must be included in a well-designed training program.
A combination of plyometrics and resistance training during a training cycle should be structured to allow maximal efficiency and physical improvement.
Combine lower body resistance training with upper body plyometrics, and upper body resistance training with lower body plyometrics.
Performing heavy resistance training and plyometrics on the same day is not usually recommended.
Some athletes may benefit from complex training, a combination of high-intensity resistance training followed by plyometrics.
If athletes perform this type of training, adequate recovery is needed between plyometrics and other high-intensity training.
Traditional resistance training exercises may be combined with plyometric movements to further enhance gains in muscular power.
For example, performing a squat jump with approximately 30% of one’s squat 1-repetition maximum (1RM) as an external resistance further increases performance.
This is an advanced form of complex training that is appropriate only for athletes who have previously participated in high-intensity plyometric training programs.
Many sports—such as basketball and soccer—have both an anaerobic (i.e., power) and an aerobic component.
Therefore, multiple types of training must be combined to best prepare athletes for these types of sports.
Because aerobic exercise may have a negative effect on power production, it is advisable to perform plyometric exercise before aerobic endurance training.
Plyometric exercise is not inherently dangerous; however, as with all modes of exercise, the risk of injury exists.

Injuries can occur simply due to an accident, but they more typically occur when proper training procedures are violated and may be the result of an insufficient strength and conditioning base, inadequate warm-up, improper progression of lead-up drills, inappropriate volume or intensity for the phase of training, poor shoes or surface, or a simple lack of skill.

To reduce the risk of injury and facilitate the performance of plyometric exercises, the athlete must understand proper plyometric technique and possess a sufficient base of strength, speed, and balance.
In addition, the athlete must be sufficiently mature, both physically and psychologically, to participate in a plyometric training program.

Before adding any drill to an athlete’s plyometric program, the strength and conditioning professional must demonstrate proper technique to the athlete in order to maximize the drill’s effectiveness and minimize the risk of injury.
For lower body plyometrics, proper landing technique is essential, particularly for depth jumps.
If the center of gravity is offset from the base of support during a landing, performance is hindered and injury may occur.
The shoulders should be over the knees during the landing, which the jumper accomplishes through flexion of the ankles, knees, and hips.
Consideration of the athlete’s level of strength is necessary before he or she performs plyometrics.
For lower body plyometrics, the athlete’s 1RM squat should be at least 1.5 times his or her body weight.

For upper body plyometrics, the bench press 1RM should be at least the athlete’s body weight for larger athletes (those weighing over 220 pounds, or 100 kg) and at least 1.5 times the body weight for smaller athletes (those weighing less than 220 pounds).
An alternative measure of prerequisite upper body strength is the ability to perform five clap push-ups in a row.
These should be considered minimal standards for performing high-intensity plyometrics.
For athletes who do not possess sufficient muscular strength or a sufficient fitness level, plyometrics should be delayed until they meet minimum standards.
Because plyometric exercise involves quick movements, the ability to move rapidly is essential before one begins a plyometric program.
For lower body plyometrics, the athlete should be able to perform five repetitions of the squat with 60% body weight in 5 seconds or less.
To meet this speed prerequisite, participation in sprint training and speed-specific resistance training programs is helpful.
To satisfy the speed requirement for upper body plyometrics, the athlete should be able to perform five repetitions of the bench press with 60% body weight in 5 seconds or less.
A less obvious lower body plyometric requirement is balance.
Balance is the maintenance of a position without moving for a given period of time.

Many lower body plyometric drills require the athlete to move in nontraditional patterns (e.g., double-leg zigzag hop and backward skip) or on a single leg (e.g., single-leg tuck jump and single-leg hop).
These types of drills necessitate a solid, stable base of support upon which the athlete can safely and correctly perform the exercises.
Three balance tests are provided in table 16.7, listed in order of difficulty; each test position must be held for 30 seconds.
For example, an athlete beginning plyometric training for the first time would be required to stand on one leg for 30 seconds without falling.
An experienced athlete beginning an advanced plyometric training program must maintain a single-leg half squat for 30 seconds without falling.
The surface on which the balance testing is performed must be the same as that used in the plyometric drills.
Plyometric training utilizes shock-absorbing surfaces.
Grass fields, suspended floors, or rubber mats are good surfaces for plyometric training (32).
Concrete, tile, and hardwood are not recommended surfaces for plyometric training because they lack effective shock-absorbing properties (32).
Excessively thick exercise mats (6 inches [15 cm] or thicker) may extend the amortization phase and thus not allow efficient use of the stretch reflex.
Mini-trampolines are commonly used for beginning plyometric and balance training in rehabilitation (28).

Mini-trampolines, like thick exercise mats, are not effective for plyometric training of uninjured athletes because the amortization phase is extended while the athlete is in contact with the elastic surface.
The amount of space needed for plyometric training depends on the drill.
Most bounding and running drills require at least 30 m (33 yards) of straightaway, though some drills may require a straightaway of 100 m (109 yards).
Most standing, box, and depth jumps only require a minimal surface area, but the ceiling height must be 3 to 4 m (9.8-13.1 feet) in order to be adequate.
Boxes used for box jumps and depth jumps must be sturdy and have a nonslip top.
Boxes should range in height from 6 to 42 inches (15 to 107 cm) and should have landing surfaces of at least 18 by 24 inches (46 by 61 cm) (16).
Boxes should be constructed of sturdy wood (e.g., 3/4-inch [1.9 cm] plywood) or heavy-gauge metal.
Ways to make the landing surface nonslip include adding nonslip treads, mixing sand into the paint used to cover the box, or affixing rubberized flooring to the top (16).
Participants must use footwear with good ankle and foot support; good lateral stability; and a wide, nonslip sole (43).
Shoes with a narrow sole and poor upper support (e.g., running shoes) may invite ankle problems, especially with lateral movements.
Shoes with insufficient foot support may lead to arch and/or lower leg injuries, while footwear without enough cushioning might lead to damage of more proximal joints (e.g., knee and hip joints).
Athletes who weigh more than 220 pounds (100 kg) may be at an increased risk for injury when performing plyometric exercises (43, 51).
Greater weight increases the compressive force on joints during plyometric exercises, thereby predisposing these joints to injury.
Athletes weighing over 220 pounds (100 kg) should avoid high-volume, high-intensity plyometric exercises.
Athletes weighing over 220 pounds should not perform depth jumps from heights greater than 18 inches (46 cm) (43, 51).
An athlete’s joint structure and previous injuries must be examined before he or she begins a plyometric training program.
Previous injuries or abnormalities of the spine, lower extremities, or upper extremities may increase an athlete’s risk of injury during plyometric exercise.

Athletes with a history of muscle strains, pathological joint laxity, or spinal dysfunction—including vertebral disk dysfunction or compression—should use caution when beginning a plyometric training program (24, 25, 32, 47).
The landing surface used for lower body plyometrics must possess adequate shock-absorbing properties to prevent injuries.
Close monitoring of athletes is necessary to ensure proper technique during plyometric exercise.
Plyometric exercise is not intrinsically dangerous when performed correctly; but as with other forms of training, poor technique may unnecessarily predispose an athlete to injury.
There is a limit to the maximal height at which a depth jump can be effectively and safely performed.
A height of 48 inches (1.2 m) would provide a significant overload on the muscles, but the resistance may be too great for many athletes to overcome while maintaining correct technique (40).

Jumping from a height of 48 inches (1.2 m) increases the possibility of injury; furthermore, the amount of force to be overcome is so great that the amortization phase is extended and thus the purpose of the exercise defeated.
The recommended height for depth jumps ranges from 16 to 42 inches (41 to 107 cm), with 30 to 32 inches (76 to 81 cm) being the norm (4, 18, 26, 37, 38, 41).
Depth jump box height for athletes who weigh over 220 pounds (100 kg) should be 18 inches (46 cm) or less.
The major goal of plyometric training is to rapidly apply force to provide an overload to the agonist muscles.
Plyometric exercise has been repeatedly shown to increase muscular power for participants in a formal training program (30, 46, 53, 54).
Research has yet to determine whether mechanical or neurophysiological adaptations account for the improvement in muscular power from plyometric exercise.
Plyometrics should be considered not an end in itself, but part of an overall program that includes strength, speed, aerobic and flexibility training, and proper nutrition.
After the athlete has begun a proper strength and conditioning program, plyometric training may be used to further develop power.

The steps for implementing a plyometric program are: 1. Evaluate the athlete. 2. Ensure that facilities and equipment are safe. 3. Establish sport-specific goals. 4. Determine program design variables. 5. Teach the athlete proper technique. 6. Properly progress the program.
Plyometric Training is on page 427.
Two-Foot Ankle Hop is a lower body plyometric drill.
The intensity level of the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is low.
The direction of the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is vertical.
The starting position for the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
The arm action for the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is none or double arm.
The preparatory movement for the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is a slight countermovement.
The upward movement for the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is hopping up with primary motion at the ankle joint.
The downward movement for the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is landing in the starting position and immediately repeating the hop.
The Two-Foot Ankle Hop should be performed with little horizontal or lateral movement.
The Squat Jump is a lower body plyometric drill.
The intensity level of the Squat Jump is low.
The direction of the Squat Jump is vertical.
The starting position for the Squat Jump is a squat position (thighs slightly above parallel with the ground) with feet shoulder-width apart.
The arm action for the Squat Jump is none.
The preparatory movement for the Squat Jump is none.
The upward movement for the Squat Jump is explosively jumping up to a maximum height.
The downward movement for the Squat Jump is landing in the squat position and immediately repeating the jump.
The Jump and Reach is a lower body plyometric drill.
The intensity level of the Jump and Reach is low.
The direction of the Jump and Reach is vertical.
The starting position for the Jump and Reach is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
The arm action for the Jump and Reach is double arm with reach at the top of the jump.
The preparatory movement for the Jump and Reach is a countermovement.
The upward movement for the Jump and Reach is explosively jumping up and reaching for an object or target.
The downward movement for the Jump and Reach is landing in the starting position and immediately repeating the jump.
Emphasis is on vertical height with minimal delay between jumps.
The Jump and Reach should be performed with little horizontal or lateral movement.
The Double-Leg Tuck Jump is a lower body plyometric drill.
The intensity level of the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is medium.
The direction of the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is vertical.
The starting position for the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
The arm action for the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is double arm.
The preparatory movement for the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is a countermovement.
The upward movement for the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is explosively jumping up. Pulling the knees to the chest, quickly grasping the knees with both hands, and releasing before landing.
The downward movement for the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is landing in the starting position and immediately repeating the jump.
SPlIT Squ AT JumP is a jump exercise with a medium intensity level.
The direction of the jump in SPlIT Squ AT JumP is vertical.
The starting position for SPlIT Squ AT JumP is a lunge position with one leg forward (hip and knee joints flexed approximately 90°) and the other behind the midline of the body.
Arm action for SPlIT Squ AT JumP is double arm or none.
The preparatory movement for SPlIT Squ AT JumP is a countermovement.
During the upward movement of SPlIT Squ AT JumP, explosively jump up, using the arms to assist as needed. Maximum height and power should be emphasized.
During the downward movement of SPlIT Squ AT JumP, maintain the lunge position (same leg forward) and immediately repeat the jump.
After completing a set of SPlIT Squ AT JumP, rest and switch front legs.
c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP is a jump exercise with a high intensity level.
The direction of the jump in c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP is vertical.
The starting position for c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP is a lunge position with one leg forward (hip and knee joints flexed approximately 90°) and the other behind the midline of the body.
Arm action for c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP is double arm or none.
The preparatory movement for c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP is a countermovement.

During the upward movement of c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP, explosively jump up, using the arms to assist as needed. While off the ground, switch the position of the legs. Maximum height and power should be emphasized.
During the downward movement of c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP, maintain the lunge position (opposite leg forward) and immediately repeat the jump.
Be sure the lunge is not too deep (as in the far right photo) in c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP, as the SSC may not be able to effectively contribute to subsequent jumps.
SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P is a jump exercise with a high intensity level.
The direction of the jump in SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P is vertical.
The starting position for SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P is a comfortable, upright stance on one foot. The nonjumping leg is held in a stationary position with the knee flexed during the exercise.
Arm action for SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P is double arm.
The preparatory movement for SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P is a countermovement.
During the upward movement of SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P, explosively jump up. Pull the knee of the jumping leg to the chest, grasp the knee with both hands, and release before landing.
During the downward movement of SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P, land in the starting position and immediately repeat the jump using the same leg.
Repeat with the opposite leg after a brief rest in SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P.
PIke Jum P is a jump exercise with a high intensity level.
The direction of the jump in PIke Jum P is vertical.
The starting position for PIke Jum P is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
Arm action for PIke Jum P is double arm.
The preparatory movement for PIke Jum P is a countermovement.
During the upward movement of PIke Jum P, explosively jump up. Keeping the legs straight and together, try to lift them to the front and try to touch the toes with the hands.
During the downward movement of PIke Jum P, land in the starting position and immediately repeat the jump.
Double-leg VerTI c Al Jum P is a jump exercise with a low intensity level.
The direction of the jump in Double-leg VerTI c Al Jum P is vertical.
The starting position for Double-leg VerTI c Al Jum P is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
Arm action for Double-leg VerTI c Al Jum P is double arm.
The preparatory movement for Double-leg VerTI c Al Jum P is a countermovement.
During the upward movement of Double-leg VerTI c Al Jum P, explosively jump up, using both arms to assist, and reach for a target.
During the downward movement of Double-leg VerTI c Al Jum P, land in the starting position and repeat the jump. Allow recovery time between jumps.
Jum P oVer bArr Ier is a jump exercise with a medium intensity level.
The direction of the jump in Jum P oVer bArr Ier is horizontal and vertical.
Equipment for Jum P oVer bArr Ier is a barrier such as a cone or hurdle.
The starting position for Jum P oVer bArr Ier is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
Arm action for Jum P oVer bArr Ier is double arm.
The preparatory movement for Jum P oVer bArr Ier is a countermovement.

During the upward movement of Jum P oVer bArr Ier, jump over a barrier with both legs, using primarily hip and knee flexion to clear the barrier. Keep the knees and feet together without lateral deviation.
During the downward movement of Jum P oVer bArr Ier, land in the starting position and repeat the jump. Allow recovery time between jumps.
The height of the barrier should be progressively increased (e.g., from a cone to a hurdle) in Jum P oVer bArr Ier.
Single-leg Vertical Jump has a high intensity level.
The direction of the Single-leg Vertical Jump is vertical.
The starting position for the Single-leg Vertical Jump is a comfortable, upright stance on one foot.
The nonjumping leg in the Single-leg Vertical Jump is held stationary with the knee flexed.
The arm action for the Single-leg Vertical Jump is double arm.
The preparatory movement for the Single-leg Vertical Jump is a countermovement.
The upward movement of the Single-leg Vertical Jump involves explosively jumping up using both arms to assist and reaching for a target.
The downward movement of the Single-leg Vertical Jump is landing in the starting position and repeating the jump using the same leg.
Recovery time is allowed between jumps in the Single-leg Vertical Jump.
The opposite leg is used after a brief rest in the Single-leg Vertical Jump.
Double-leg Hop has a medium intensity level.
The direction of the Double-leg Hop is horizontal and vertical.
The starting position for the Double-leg Hop is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
The arm action for the Double-leg Hop is double arm.
The preparatory movement for the Double-leg Hop is a countermovement.
The upward movement of the Double-leg Hop involves jumping as far forward as possible.
The downward movement of the Double-leg Hop is landing in the starting position and immediately repeating the hop.
Double-leg Zigzag Hop has a high intensity level.
The direction of the Double-leg Zigzag Hop is diagonal.
Equipment for the Double-leg Zigzag Hop includes about 10 hurdles placed 18 to 24 inches (45-60 cm) apart in a zigzag pattern.
The starting position for the Double-leg Zigzag Hop is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart, standing on the outside of the first hurdle.
Elbows should be flexed at 90° and held at the sides of the body in the Double-leg Zigzag Hop.
The arm action for the Double-leg Zigzag Hop is double arm.
The preparatory movement for the Double-leg Zigzag Hop is a countermovement.

The upward movement of the Double-leg Zigzag Hop involves jumping from the outside of the first hurdle to the outside of the second hurdle, keeping the shoulders perpendicular to an imaginary line through the center of all hurdles.

The downward movement of the Double-leg Zigzag Hop is immediately upon landing on the outside of the second hurdle, changing direction and jumping diagonally over the second hurdle to the outside of the third hurdle.
The Double-leg Zigzag Hop involves continuing to hop over all the hurdles.
A less intense version of the Double-leg Zigzag Hop involves setting the hurdles in a straight line and hopping over one hurdle at a time.
The intensity of the Double-leg Zigzag Hop can be increased by performing the hops with one leg only.
Single-leg Hop has a high intensity level.
The direction of the Single-leg Hop is horizontal and vertical.
The starting position for the Single-leg Hop is a comfortable, upright stance on one foot.
The nonjumping leg in the Single-leg Hop is held stationary with the knee flexed.
The arm action for the Single-leg Hop is double arm.
The preparatory movement for the Single-leg Hop is a countermovement.
The upward movement of the Single-leg Hop involves explosively jumping forward using both arms to assist.
Front Barrier Hop has a medium intensity level.
The direction of the Front Barrier Hop is horizontal and vertical.
Equipment for the Front Barrier Hop includes two barriers such as two cones or two hurdles.
The starting position for the Front Barrier Hop is facing the first barrier, getting into a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
The arm action for the Front Barrier Hop is double arm.
The preparatory movement for the Front Barrier Hop is a countermovement.
The upward movement of the Front Barrier Hop involves jumping over the first barrier with both legs, using primarily hip and knee flexion to clear the barrier.
The knees and feet should be kept together without lateral deviation during the Front Barrier Hop.
The downward movement of the Front Barrier Hop is landing in the starting position and immediately repeating the jump over a second barrier.

The intensity level of the Front Barrier Hop can be increased from medium to high by progressively increasing the height of the barrier (e.g., from a cone to a hurdle) or by performing the hops with one leg only.
The lateral barrier hop has a medium intensity level.
The direction of the lateral barrier hop is lateral and vertical.
Equipment for the lateral barrier hop includes a barrier such as a cone or hurdle.
The starting position for the lateral barrier hop is with the barrier to one side, in a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
The arm action for the lateral barrier hop is double arm.
The preparatory movement for the lateral barrier hop is a countermovement.
The upward movement of the lateral barrier hop involves jumping over the barrier with both legs, using primarily hip and knee flexion to clear the barrier.
During the lateral barrier hop, the knees and feet should be kept together.
The downward movement of the lateral barrier hop involves landing on the opposite side of the barrier and immediately repeating the jump to the starting side.

The intensity level of the lateral barrier hop can be increased from medium to high by progressively increasing the height of the barrier (e.g., from a cone to a hurdle) or by performing the hops with one leg only.
The skip has a low intensity level.
The direction of the skip is horizontal and vertical.
The starting position for the skip is with one leg lifted to approximately 90° of hip and knee flexion.
The arm action for the skip is reciprocal (as one leg is lifted, the opposite arm is lifted).
The preparatory movement for the skip is a countermovement on one leg.
The upward movement of the skip involves jumping up and forward on one leg. The opposite leg should remain in the starting flexed position until landing.
The downward movement of the skip involves landing in the starting position on the same leg. Immediately repeat the skip with the opposite leg.
The power skip has a low intensity level.
The direction of the power skip is vertical and horizontal.
The starting position for the power skip is with one leg lifted to approximately 90° of hip and knee flexion.
The arm action for the power skip is double arm.
The preparatory movement for the power skip is a countermovement on one leg.

The upward movement of the power skip involves jumping up and forward on one leg. Move the flexed, nonjumping leg up and into greater hip and knee flexion while jumping. Both arms should be used to assist with the upward movement.
The downward movement of the power skip involves landing in the starting position on the same leg. Immediately repeat the skip with the opposite leg.
Emphasis is on the effectiveness of the skip for the power skip.
The backward skip has a low intensity level.
The direction of the backward skip is backward, horizontal, and vertical.
The starting position for the backward skip is with one leg lifted to approximately 90° of hip and knee flexion.
The arm action for the backward skip is double arm.
The preparatory movement for the backward skip is a countermovement on one leg.

The upward movement of the backward skip involves jumping backward with one leg and flexing the hip and knee of the nonskipping leg to approximately 90°. Both arms should be used to assist with the movement.
The downward movement of the backward skip involves landing in the starting position on the same leg. Immediately repeat the skip with the opposite leg.
The single-arm alternate-leg bound has a medium intensity level.
The direction of the single-arm alternate-leg bound is horizontal and vertical.
The starting position for the single-arm alternate-leg bound is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
The arm action for the single-arm alternate-leg bound is single arm.
The preparatory movement for the single-arm alternate-leg bound is jogging at a comfortable pace; begin the drill with the left foot forward.

The upward movement of the single-arm alternate-leg bound involves pushing off with the left foot as it contacts the ground. During push-off, bring the right leg forward by flexing the thigh to a position approximately parallel with the ground and the knee at 90°. During this flight phase of the drill, reach forward with the left arm.
The downward movement of the single-arm alternate-leg bound involves landing on the right leg and immediately repeating the sequence on the opposite side upon landing.
A bound is an exaggeration of the running gait; the goal is to cover as great a distance as possible during each stride.
Double-Arm Alternate-leg Bound involves a position approximately parallel with the ground and with the knee at 90°.
During the flight phase of the Double-Arm Alternate-leg Bound, reach forward with both arms.
The downward movement of the Double-Arm Alternate-leg Bound involves landing on the right leg and immediately repeating the sequence on the opposite side upon landing.
A bound is an exaggeration of the running gait with the goal of covering as great a distance as possible during each stride.
The intensity level of the Double-Arm Alternate-leg Bound is medium.
The direction of the jump in the Double-Arm Alternate-leg Bound is horizontal and vertical.
The starting position for the Double-Arm Alternate-leg Bound is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
The arm action for the Double-Arm Alternate-leg Bound is double arm.
The preparatory movement for the Double-Arm Alternate-leg Bound is jogging at a comfortable pace; begin the drill with the left foot forward.
The upward movement of the Double-Arm Alternate-leg Bound involves pushing off with the left foot as it contacts the ground and bringing the right leg forward by flexing the thigh.
Single-leg Push-off involves landing with the same foot on the box; this foot should land just before the ground foot.
The intensity level of the Single-leg Push-off is low.
The direction of the jump in the Single-leg Push-off is vertical.
Equipment for the Single-leg Push-off includes a plyometric box, 6 to 18 inches (15-46 cm) high.
The starting position for the Single-leg Push-off is standing facing the plyometric box with one foot on the ground and one foot on the box.
The heel of the foot on the box in the Single-leg Push-off should be near the box’s closest edge.
The arm action for the Single-leg Push-off is double arm.
There is no preparatory movement for the Single-leg Push-off.
The upward movement of the Single-leg Push-off involves jumping up using the foot on the box to push off.
Alternate-leg Push-off involves landing with the opposite foot on the box; this foot should land just before the ground foot.
The intensity level of the Alternate-leg Push-off is low.
The direction of the jump in the Alternate-leg Push-off is vertical.
Equipment for the Alternate-leg Push-off includes a plyometric box, 6 to 18 inches (15-46 cm) high.
The starting position for the Alternate-leg Push-off is standing facing the plyometric box with one foot on the ground and one foot on the box.
The heel of the foot on the box in the Alternate-leg Push-off should be near the box’s closest edge.
The arm action for the Alternate-leg Push-off is double arm.
There is no preparatory movement for the Alternate-leg Push-off.
The upward movement of the Alternate-leg Push-off involves jumping up using the foot on the box to push off.
Lateral Push-off involves landing with the same foot on the box; this foot should land just before the ground foot.
The intensity level of the Lateral Push-off is low.
The direction of the jump in the Lateral Push-off is vertical.
Equipment for the Lateral Push-off includes a plyometric box, 6 to 18 inches (15-46 cm) high.
The starting position for the Lateral Push-off is standing to one side of the plyometric box with one foot on the ground and one foot on the box.
The inside of the foot on the box in the Lateral Push-off should be near the box’s closest edge.
The arm action for the Lateral Push-off is double arm.
There is no preparatory movement for the Lateral Push-off.
The upward movement of the Lateral Push-off involves jumping up using the foot on the box to push off.
Side-to-Side Push-off involves jumping up and over the box using the foot on the box to push off.
The intensity level of the Side-to-Side Push-off is medium.
The direction of the jump in the Side-to-Side Push-off is vertical.
Equipment for the Side-to-Side Push-off includes a plyometric box, 6 to 18 inches (15-46 cm) high.
The starting position for the Side-to-Side Push-off is standing to one side of the plyometric box with one foot on the ground and one foot on the box.
The inside of the foot on the box in the Side-to-Side Push-off should be near the box’s closest edge.
The arm action for the Side-to-Side Push-off is double arm.
There is no preparatory movement for the Side-to-Side Push-off.
The downward movement of the Side-to-Side Push-off involves landing with the opposite foot on the opposite side of the top of the box; this foot should land just before the ground foot.
Intensity may be increased for all box drills by increasing the height of the box. Begin with a height of 6 inches (15 cm).
Box Drills (continued)
Jum P To b ox
Intensity level: Low
Direction of jump: Vertical and slightly horizontal
Equipment: Plyometric box, 6 to 42 inches (15-107 cm) high
Starting position: Facing the plyometric box, get into a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
Arm action: Double arm
Preparatory movement: Begin with a countermovement.
Upward movement: Jump onto the top of the box using both legs.
Downward movement: Land on both feet in a half squat position, step down from the box, and repeat.
Squ AT box Jum P
Intensity level: Medium
Direction of jump: Vertical and slightly horizontal
Equipment: Plyometric box, 6 to 42 inches (15-107 cm) high
Starting position: Facing the plyometric box with hands clasped behind head, get into a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
Arm action: None
Preparatory movement: Begin with a countermovement.
Upward movement: Jump onto the top of the box using both legs.
Downward movement: Land on both feet in a half squat position, step down from the box, and repeat.
Note: Intensity may be increased by increasing the height of the box. Begin with a height of 6 inches (15 cm).
lATer Al b ox Jum P
Intensity level: Medium
Direction of jump: Vertical and slightly horizontal
Equipment: Plyometric box, 6 to 42 inches (15-107 cm) high
Starting position: Stand to one side of the plyometric box; get into a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart.
Arm action: Double arm
Preparatory movement: Begin with a countermovement.
Upward movement: Jump onto the top of the box using both legs.
Downward movement: Land on both feet in a half squat position, step down from the box, and repeat in the opposite direction.
Note: Intensity may be increased by increasing the height of the box. Begin with a height of 6 inches (15 cm).
Jum P From b ox
Intensity level: Medium
Direction of jump: Vertical
Equipment: Plyometric box, 12 to 42 inches (30-107 cm) high
Starting position: Get into a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart on the plyometric box; toes should be near the edge of the box.
Arm action: None
Preparatory movement: Step from box.
Downward movement: Land on the floor with both feet, quickly absorbing the impact upon landing. Step back onto the box and repeat.
Note: Intensity may be increased by increasing the height of the box. Begin with a height of 12 inches (30 cm).
Depth Jumps
DePTH Jum P
Intensity level: High
Direction of jump: Vertical
Equipment: Plyometric box, 12 to 42 inches (30-107 cm) high
Starting position: Get into a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart on the plyometric box; toes should be near the edge of the box.
Arm action: Double arm
Preparatory movement: Step from box.
Downward movement: Land on the floor with both feet.
Upward movement: Upon landing, immediately jump up as high as possible.

Note: When stepping from the box, step straight out. Do not first jump up or lower your center of gravity as you step down, as these adjustments will change the height from which the exercise is performed.
Note: Time on the ground should be kept to a minimum. Intensity may be increased by increasing the height of the box. Begin with a height of 12 inches (30 cm).
Note: Upon landing, emphasis should be on jumping up, with minimal horizontal movement. The far right photo shows too much forward movement.
Step  from box
Depth Jumps (continued)
DePTH Jum P To Secon D box
Intensity level: High
Direction of jump: Vertical and horizontal
Equipment: Two plyometric boxes, 12 to 42 inches (30-107 cm) high
Starting position: Get into a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart on the plyometric box, facing the second box; toes should be near the edge of the box.
Arm action: Double arm
Preparatory movement: Step from box.
Downward movement: Land on the floor with both feet.
Upward movement: Upon landing, immediately jump onto the second box.

Note: When stepping from the box, step straight out. Do not first jump up or lower your center of gravity as you step down, as these adjustments will change the height from which the exercise is performed.
Note: Time on the ground should be kept to a minimum. Intensity may be increased by increasing the height of the box. Begin with a height of 12 inches (30 cm).
Note: The distance between boxes depends on experience and ability; the greater the distance between boxes, the higher the intensity of the jump. Begin with the boxes placed 24 inches (61 cm) apart.
Depth Jumps (continued)
Squ AT DePTH Jum P
Intensity level: High
Direction of jump: Vertical
Equipment: Plyometric box, 12 to 42 inches (30-107 cm) high
Starting position: Get into a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart on the plyometric box; toes should be near the edge of the box.
Arm action: Double arm or none
Preparatory movement: Step from box.
Downward movement: Land on the floor in a squat position (90° of hip and knee flexion) with both feet. (The subject in the center photo should have greater hip and knee flexion.)
Upward movement: Upon landing, immediately jump up as high as possible; land in the same squat position.

Note: When stepping from the box, step straight out. Do not first jump up or lower your center of gravity as you step down, as these adjustments will change the height from which the exercise is performed.
Note: Time on the ground should be kept to a minimum. Intensity may be increased by increasing the height of the box. Begin with a height of 12 inches (30 cm).
Note: Upon landing, emphasis should be on jumping up, with minimal horizontal movement.
DePTH Jum P WITH lATer Al m oVemenT
Intensity level: High
Direction of jump: Vertical and lateral
Equipment: Plyometric box, 12 to 42 inches (30-107 cm) high; a partner
Starting position: Get into a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart on the plyometric box; toes should be near the edge of the box.
Arm action: Double arm
Preparatory movement: Step from box.
Downward movement: Land on the floor with both feet. Have a partner point to the right or left just before you land.
Upward movement: Upon landing, immediately sprint in the direction determined by your partner.

Note: When stepping from the box, step straight out. Do not first jump up or lower your center of gravity as you step down, as these adjustments will change the height from which the exercise is performed.
Note: Time on the ground should be kept to a minimum. Intensity may be increased by increasing the height of the box. Begin with a height of 12 inches (30 cm).
Depth Jumps (continued) is a plyometric exercise.
The Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump has a high intensity level.
The Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump involves a vertical and horizontal direction of jump.
Equipment for the Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump includes a plyometric box that is 12 to 42 inches (30-107 cm) high.
The starting position for the Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart on the plyometric box; toes should be near the edge of the box.
The arm action for the Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump is double arm.
The preparatory movement for the Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump is to step from the box.
The downward movement for the Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump is to land on the floor with both feet.
The upward movement for the Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump is to immediately jump forward as far as possible with both feet upon landing.
When stepping from the box for the Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump, step straight out.
Do not jump up or lower your center of gravity as you step down for the Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump, as these adjustments will change the height from which the exercise is performed.
Time on the ground should be kept to a minimum for the Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump.
Intensity for the Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump may be increased by increasing the height of the box.
Begin with a height of 12 inches (30 cm) for the Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump.
The Single-Leg Depth Jump has a high intensity level.
The Single-Leg Depth Jump involves a vertical direction of jump.
Equipment for the Single-Leg Depth Jump includes a plyometric box that is 12 to 42 inches (30-107 cm) high.
The starting position for the Single-Leg Depth Jump is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart on the plyometric box; toes should be near the edge of the box.
The arm action for the Single-Leg Depth Jump is double arm.
The preparatory movement for the Single-Leg Depth Jump is to step from the box.
The downward movement for the Single-Leg Depth Jump is to land on the floor with one foot.
The upward movement for the Single-Leg Depth Jump is to immediately jump up as high as possible with the landing foot upon landing.
When stepping from the box for the Single-Leg Depth Jump, step straight out.
Do not jump up or lower your center of gravity as you step down for the Single-Leg Depth Jump, as these adjustments will change the height from which the exercise is performed.
Time on the ground should be kept to a minimum for the Single-Leg Depth Jump.
Intensity for the Single-Leg Depth Jump may be increased by increasing the height of the box.
Begin with a height of 12 inches (30 cm) for the Single-Leg Depth Jump.
The Single-Leg Depth Jump is a very advanced form of the depth jump and should be performed only by those with adequate experience and ability as demonstrated in other versions of the depth jump.
Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws has a low intensity level.
The direction of throw for Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws is forward.
Equipment for Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws includes a medicine or plyometric ball weighing 2 to 8 pounds (0.9-3.6 kg); rebounder or partner.

The starting position for Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart; face the rebounder or partner approximately 10 feet (3 m) away. Raise the ball to chest level with the elbows flexed.

The preparatory movement for Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws is to begin with a countermovement. (A countermovement for plyometric throws requires cocking the arms, that is, moving the arms slightly backward before the actual throw.)

The arm action for Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws is to use both arms, throw the ball to the rebounder or partner by extending the elbows. When the rebounder or partner returns the ball, catch it, return to the starting position, and immediately repeat the movement.
Intensity for Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws may be increased by increasing the weight of the medicine ball. Begin with a 2-pound (0.9 kg) ball.
The Chest Pass is a type of Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws.
The Two-Handed Overhead Throw has a low intensity level.
The direction of throw for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw is forward.
Equipment for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw includes a medicine or plyometric ball weighing 2 to 8 pounds (0.9-3.6 kg); rebounder or partner.

The starting position for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart; face the rebounder or partner approximately 10 feet (3 m) away. Raise the ball overhead.

The preparatory movement for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw is to begin with a countermovement. (A countermovement for plyometric throws requires cocking the arms, that is, moving the arms slightly backward before the actual throw.)

The arm action for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw is to use both arms, throw the ball to the rebounder or partner, keeping the elbows extended. When the rebounder or partner returns the ball, catch the ball overhead and immediately repeat the throw.
Intensity for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw may be increased by increasing the weight of the medicine ball. Begin with a 2-pound (0.9 kg) ball.
The Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw has a low intensity level.
The Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw is thrown forward and diagonally.
Equipment for the Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw includes a medicine or plyometric ball weighing 2 to 8 pounds (0.9-3.6 kg) and a rebounder or partner.
The starting position for the Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart, facing the rebounder or partner approximately 10 feet (3 m) away.
In the Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw, the ball is raised in both hands to a position over one shoulder with the elbows flexed.
A countermovement for the Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw involves cocking the arms, moving them slightly backward before the throw.
The Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw involves throwing the ball to the rebounder or partner by extending the elbows.
The intensity of the Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw can be increased by increasing the weight of the medicine ball.
The starting weight for the Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw is a 2-pound (0.9 kg) ball.
The Single-Arm Throw has a medium intensity level.
The Single-Arm Throw is thrown forward.
Equipment for the Single-Arm Throw includes a medicine or plyometric ball weighing 1 to 5 pounds (0.5-2.3 kg) and a rebounder or partner.
The starting position for the Single-Arm Throw is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart, facing the rebounder or partner approximately 10 feet (3 m) away.
In the Single-Arm Throw, the ball is raised in one hand to a position of 90° of shoulder abduction and 90° of elbow flexion, with the arm rotated so the forearm is perpendicular to the floor.
A countermovement for the Single-Arm Throw involves cocking the arm, moving it slightly backward before the throw.
The Single-Arm Throw involves throwing the ball to the rebounder or partner using one arm.
The intensity of the Single-Arm Throw can be increased by increasing the weight of the medicine ball.
The starting weight for the Single-Arm Throw is a 1-pound (0.5 kg) ball.
The Single-Arm Throw may also be performed using a natural throwing motion.
The Power Drop has a high intensity level.
The Power Drop is thrown upward.
Equipment for the Power Drop includes a medicine or plyometric ball weighing 2 to 8 pounds (0.9-3.6 kg), a partner, and a plyometric box 12 to 42 inches (30-107 cm) high.
The starting position for the Power Drop is lying supine on the ground with elbows extended and both shoulders in approximately 90° of flexion; the head should be near the base of the box.
The partner in the Power Drop should be on a box with the medicine ball held above the athlete’s arms.
The Power Drop does not require a preparatory movement.
In the Power Drop, the ball is caught using both arms when the partner drops it, and then immediately thrown back up to the partner.
The intensity of the Power Drop can be increased by increasing the weight of the medicine ball or by increasing the height of the box.
The starting weight for the Power Drop is a 2-pound (0.9 kg) ball and a height of 12 inches (30 cm).
The plyometric push-up has a medium intensity level.
The direction of movement for the plyometric push-up is vertical.
Equipment needed for the plyometric push-up includes a medicine or plyometric ball weighing 5 to 8 pounds (2.3-3.6 kg).
The starting position for the plyometric push-up is lying in a push-up position with hands on the medicine ball and elbows extended.

The downward movement of the plyometric push-up involves quickly removing the hands from the medicine ball and dropping down, contacting the ground with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart and elbows slightly flexed, allowing the chest to almost touch the medicine ball.

The upward movement of the plyometric push-up involves immediately and explosively pushing up by fully extending the elbows, quickly placing the palms on the medicine ball, and repeating the exercise.
When the upper body is at maximal height during the upward movement of the plyometric push-up, the hands should be higher than the medicine ball.
Intensity for the plyometric push-up can be increased by increasing the size of the medicine ball, starting with a 5-pound (2.3 kg) ball.
The 45° sit-up has a medium intensity level.
Equipment needed for the 45° sit-up includes a medicine or plyometric ball weighing 2 to 8 pounds (0.9-3.6 kg) and a partner.
The starting position for the 45° sit-up is sitting on the ground with the trunk at an approximately 45° angle, with the partner in front with the medicine ball.
The preparatory movement for the 45° sit-up is the partner throwing the ball to the participant's outstretched hands.
The downward action of the 45° sit-up involves catching the ball using both arms, allowing minimal trunk extension, and immediately returning the ball to the partner.
Intensity for the 45° sit-up can be increased by increasing the weight of the medicine ball, starting with a 2-pound (0.9 kg) ball.
The force used to return the ball to the partner in the 45° sit-up should come predominantly from the abdominal muscles.
This chapter addresses the issue of speed development on three fronts: speed, agility, and speed-endurance.
Speed is the skills and abilities needed to achieve high movement velocities.
Agility is the skills and abilities needed to explosively change movement velocities or modes.
Speed-endurance is the ability to maintain maximal movement velocities or repeatedly achieve maximal accelerations and velocities.
There is a common misconception that skills and abilities are unrelated, when in fact they are interrelated.
Strength and conditioning professionals should analyze the skills and abilities needed in a target activity and specifically develop them in training.
Functional movement speed is a manifestation of an athlete’s strength.
Forces are expressed in terms of acceleration, time or rate of application, and velocity.
Movement techniques involve skillful application of these forces to specific motor tasks.
The greater the mass being accelerated, the briefer the time of execution, or the higher the velocity of movement, the more explosively an athlete must apply forces in order to execute a technique.
Running is the technical example used throughout this chapter because it is a fundamental motor skill and means of locomotion in many sports.
Running also has an explosive, ballistic quality common to other movements.
The term agility is commonly used in two contexts: changing movement velocity and performing locomotion modes other than linear sprinting.
Velocity is a vector quantity characterized by speed and direction, so in a broad sense there is an agility component to any task involving changes in either parameter.
Variable, rather than constant, movement velocities and modes are the rule rather than the exception in many sports.
There is a corresponding need to address specific acceleration, maximum-velocity, and multidirectional skills in training.
The ability to explosively decelerate and reaccelerate is especially important because of the extreme forces involved in such tasks.

Speed-endurance provides the metabolic conditioning needed to support the maintenance of running speed and agility over an extended period (6+ seconds) or to achieve maximum acceleration or speed during repetitive sprints.
Relatively few athletic activities involve a single, brief effort.
Many sports consist of ongoing submaximal activity with intense, intermittent bursts in effort.
Even in brief, discrete activities, specific endurance qualities are necessary to achieve certain volume-loads in training.

It is therefore important to develop athletes’ special endurance—the ability to repeatedly perform maximal or near-maximal efforts in competition-specific exercise:relief patterns (commonly called work:rest ratios).

This is a variation on the speed-endurance concept and requires two qualities: the metabolic power to execute specific techniques at the targeted effort level, and the metabolic capacity to do so repetitively.
Movement techniques involve task-specific application of forces that are manifested in terms of acceleration, time or rate of application, and velocity.
Strength and conditioning professionals should identify the target activity’s requisite skills and abilities via task analysis and specifically address them in training.
In order to execute movement techniques, athletes must skillfully apply force—the product of mass and acceleration.

Due to the time and velocity constraints imposed by functional activities, task-specific force application can also be expressed in terms of two other parameters: impulse—the change in momentum resulting from a force, measured as the product of force and time, and power—the rate of doing work, measured as the product of force and velocity.
When the mechanics of explosive activities like running are analyzed, each of these variables influences performance.
More force is needed to accelerate a predetermined mass at a greater rate, or greater mass at a predetermined rate.
More impulse is needed to achieve a predetermined momentum in less time, or greater momentum in a predetermined time.
More power is needed to achieve a predetermined velocity with higher resistance, or higher velocity with a predetermined resistance.
The brief execution times of most athletic tasks require high rates of force development (RFD).
Force is applied for 0.1 to 0.2 seconds during the ground support phase of running, whereas absolute maximum force development may require 0.6 to 0.8 seconds.
Even in nonballistic modes of locomotion such as cycling, rowing, or swimming, performance is influenced by the ability to generate force quickly and thereby achieve a desired impulse.
In practice, the rate, amplitude, and direction of force application are equally significant in the performance of functional tasks.
A basic objective of training is to improve RFD, effectively moving the force–time curve up and to the left, in turn generating greater impulse.
Maximum power (Pm) is usually produced at 30% to 50% of maximum force (Fm) and velocity (Vm).
The greatest forces occur during explosive eccentric (lengthening) actions.
Resistance training should involve a variety of concentric and eccentric loads and velocities.
Training programs that are restricted to narrow force–velocity ranges may have limited transfer to athleticism.
Achievable movement speed for a particular task is load dependent.
As resistance increases, so does the role of strength in determining movement velocity and acceleration.
Velocity specificity is really the final movement velocity targeted when a mass is being accelerated.
In terrestrial movements such as running or jumping, resistance usually includes the athlete’s body mass and possibly equipment.
The athlete’s percentage of fast-twitch motor units and the ability to optimally activate and coordinate them primarily determine these capabilities.
Isometric or low-velocity strength is a function of muscle cross-sectional area.

Once the upper limit for specific muscle tension has been developed (40-45 N/cm2 in trained athletes), hypertrophy is required—especially in fast-twitch fibers—in order to further increase force and speed production.
The peak force and power levels absorbed by the tissues during active lengthening can be significantly greater than those produced during shortening.
For example, the eccentric ground reaction force (GRF) during sprinting can approach four times an athlete’s body weight, and Achilles tendon forces can be severalfold higher.
These forces are transmitted through a single leg and may limit an athlete’s technical proficiency or cause noncontact injury if not adequately addressed in training.

Thus the demands of athletic movements dictate two more training objectives in addition to improving concentric power production capability: developing the eccentric strength needed to tolerate extreme force and power absorption during eccentric (braking) actions and the reactive strength needed to rapidly recoil into concentric (propulsive) actions.
Athletes skillfully apply forces when executing movement techniques.
Because of time and velocity constraints, a technique can be characterized in terms of task-specific impulse and power.
The ability to achieve high movement velocities and accelerations involves high rate of force development (RFD) as well as force application across a range of power outputs and muscle actions.
Athletes’ mechanics change as they move.
Leverage changes with varying limb positions and joint angles.
The force–velocity relationship changes as athletes accelerate and decelerate.
The length–force relationship changes and elastic structures are loaded or unloaded as muscles lengthen and shorten.
These factors feed back to the neuromuscular system through reflexes that compensate for load and stretch, and in turn modify motor behavior via stiffness regulation.
Speed of execution in complex, multijoint movements is determined by an interplay of neuromuscular, mechanical, and energetic factors.
Explosiveness in such tasks can be expressed in terms of acceleration, momentum, or velocity.
These, in turn, are load-dependent functions of force, impulse, and power, respectively.
Speed in complex movements tends to correlate poorly with that in unresisted, elementary actions.
In such unloaded conditions, the peak rate of intrinsic muscle shortening is approximately 15 mm/s.

The peak rate of intrinsic muscle shortening depends on several factors: contractility and excitability of the athlete’s neuromuscular system, muscle architecture, and motor unit composition and maximal cross-bridge cycling rates in respective fiber types.

Many functional tasks begin with preparatory countermovements involving springlike actions referred to as the stretch-shortening cycle (SSC)—an eccentric-concentric coupling phenomenon in which muscle-tendon complexes are rapidly and forcibly lengthened, or stretch loaded, and immediately shortened in a reactive or elastic manner.
Stretch-shortening cycle actions are particularly prevalent in sports involving running, jumping, and other explosive changes in momentum or velocity.
Their performance is a distinct capability that is independent of maximal strength in elite athletes.
Stretch-shortening actions exploit two phenomena: intrinsic muscle-tendon behavior and force and length reflex feedback to the motoneural system.

Acutely, SSC actions tend to increase mechanical efficiency, impulse, and power via elastic energy recovery, whereas chronically, they upregulate muscle stiffness and enhance neuromuscular activation.
Training activities aimed at improving SSC performance should involve skillful, multijoint movements that transmit forces through the kinetic chain and exploit elastic-reflexive mechanisms.

Training activities aimed at improving SSC performance should be structured around brief work bouts or clusters separated by frequent rest pauses in order to manage fatigue and emphasize work quality and technique.
A combination of progressive plyometric and heavy resistance methods can accomplish these objectives.
Complex training, in which alternating SSC tasks with heavy resistance exercises within the same session enhances their working effect, is an intriguing example of this strategy.
The basis of this method is an acute aftereffect phenomenon referred to as postactivation potentiation.
It is becoming increasingly popular as a means of enhancing advanced athletes’ performance, but may be inappropriate for novices or youths.
Reactive ability is a characteristic of explosive strength exhibited in SSC actions that can be improved through reactive-explosive training.
Reaction time is relatively untrainable and correlates poorly with movement action time or performance in many explosive events.
An elite sprinter’s auditory reaction time typically ranges from 0.12 to 0.18 seconds but is not significantly related to his or her 100 m results.
Other factors such as acceleration, speed-endurance, and, to a lesser extent, maximum speed are more closely associated with overall sprint times.
Reaction time is an important determinant of performance in quick timing tasks (e.g., a batter hitting a baseball) and defensive types of stimulus-response actions (e.g., a goaltender making a save).
Explosive strength qualities are important in power sports involving running, jumping, and other changes in speed or direction.
Elite marathoners’ running velocities are about half those achieved by sprinters.
Ground contact times at intermediate running speeds tend to be longer than those at top speeds (~0.2 seconds at 5-6 m/s).
Ground contact times are significantly shorter than required for maximal force development.
Because running is an inherently ballistic activity, power, impulse, and reactive ability are important determinants of performance over any distance or duration.

The value of explosive strength for long-distance aerobic endurance events becomes apparent when one considers the improvements that are achievable with modest increases in stride frequency, length, or efficiency.
The ability to apply force rapidly—as well as to accelerate and decelerate one’s body mass—is the rule rather than the exception in athletics.
Running is an explosive activity requiring high RFD and power output at all velocities.
Even during prolonged steady-state running, athletes apply GRFs in a series of brief spikes.
It is simplistic to categorize any sport on a physiologic basis without giving equal attention to task-specific mechanics.
Bipedal running is a ballistic mode of locomotion with an alternating flight phase and single-leg support phase.
Walking is nonballistic; there is no flight phase, and the stance alternates between double and single support phases.
Sprinting is a series of running strides that repeatedly launch the athlete’s body as a projectile at maximal acceleration or velocity (or both), usually over brief distances and durations.
Running speed is the interaction of stride frequency and stride length.
Elite sprinters achieve greater stride length and are capable of increasing it up to ~45 m from a static start, whereas novices achieve their maximum stride length at ~25 m.

Elite sprinters achieve greater stride frequency (~5 strides per second) and are capable of increasing it up to ~25 m from a static start, whereas novices achieve their maximum stride frequency at ~10 to 15 m.

Elite sprinters produce greater initial forces and velocities at the start, achieve much greater rates of acceleration, and reach maximal velocities of up to 12 m/s after ~5 to 6 seconds (45-55 m); novices reach their top speed at 20 to 30 m.
As speed approaches maximum, frequency changes more than length and is thereby more important in determining final velocity.
Effective stride length refers to horizontal projection of the athlete’s center of mass rather than foot-to-foot touchdown distance.
Stride length is related to body height and leg length, making it unique for each individual.
Stride length is influenced by the impulse applied during ground contact and the resulting trajectory.
Stride frequency tends to vary among individuals and is generally more trainable than stride length.
As an athlete accelerates to their maximum stride rate, ground contact time decreases from ≤0.2 seconds during acceleration to ≤0.1 seconds at top speed.
At top speed, impulse production becomes increasingly dependent on the ability to generate explosive ground reaction forces (GRFs).
Linear sprinting involves the start and acceleration and maximum velocity.

Acceleration and maximum-velocity running are both characterized by two phases: flight, which includes recovery and ground preparation; and support, which includes eccentric braking and concentric propulsion.

Running is a ballistic mode of locomotion with alternating phases of flight (composed of recovery and ground preparation) and single-leg support (composed of eccentric braking and concentric propulsion).
In elite sprinters, the front knee angle is ~90° and the rear knee angle ranges from ~110° to 130° during the start.
In elite sprinters, the front hip angle is ~40° and the rear hip angle is ~80° during the start.
In subelite sprinters, the front hip angle is ~50° and the rear hip angle is ~90° during the start.
The starting torso angle is ~42° to 45° from horizontal in elite and subelite sprinters.
The rear leg produces greater initial force, but lifts off earlier (force is applied for ≤0.18 seconds in elite sprinters).
The front leg has a greater influence on starting velocity and must exert force longer (force is applied for ≤0.37 seconds in elite sprinters).
Peak starting force and impulse can exceed 1,500 newtons and 230 newton-seconds, respectively.
The front leg completes its extension as the rear leg swings forward.
As the rear foot lifts up and swings forward, the opposite arm swings forward and up; elbow flexes ~90° as hand swings toward the forehead to enhance momentum and overcome inertia.
The forward leg swing requires a backward arm countermovement.
In elite sprinters, the support phases during the first two strides are ≤0.20 and 0.18 seconds, respectively (includes eccentric braking and concentric propulsion).
In elite sprinters, the flight phases during the first two strides are ≤0.70 and 0.90 seconds, respectively.
Every athlete has an optimal stride rate:length ratio; both increase for the first 15 to 20 m, or 8 to 10 strides, from a static start.
Stride length and frequency gradually increase, up to ~45 m and ~25 m, respectively.
After the first two strides off the line, the foot touches down in front of the center of gravity.
Forward body lean progressively decreases from ≤45° to ≤5° with increasing stride rate and length; an upright sprinting position is achieved by ~20 m.
Acceleration rates can approach 11.8 m/s².
Peak GRFs can exceed 3,000 watts.
The head is in a relaxed, neutral position.
The eyes are focused straight ahead.
An extended leg and trunk form a 'power line' position along the long axis.
The thigh of the swing leg is perpendicular (with the lower leg parallel) to the trunk.
Maximum velocity is ~20 m/s in elite sprinters.
The smallest knee angle occurs when the thigh is vertical.
When the thigh reaches maximum possible knee lift, the lower leg swings forward in a relaxed movement.
The thigh begins to move down, sweeping the lower leg backward and down with a 'pawing' action.
A 'cocked' foot lightly meets the ground directly under or a minimal distance in front of the center of gravity.
In the forward support phase, body weight is balanced such that only the ball of the foot touches the ground.
The trunk is virtually erect (<5° forward lean).
Horizontal braking forces and vertical displacement are minimized in the forward support phase.
Peak GRFs are generated within 0.04 seconds in elite sprinters.
Propulsion velocity depends on the push-off impulse and direction in the rear support phase.
The 'triple extension' (hip-knee-ankle) is important; the push-off angle from the surface is ~50° to 55° in the rear support phase.
Leg drive is facilitated by explosive arm action in the rear support phase.
The shoulders are kept steady; elbows are flexed at ~90° and kept close to the body; hands are loosely cupped in the rear support phase.

In an explosive 'hammering' motion, hands swing forward and up above shoulder height (the upper arm is perpendicular to the trunk), then down and backward through the pocket and past the hips in the rear support phase.
A relaxed carriage of the head prevents facial and neck muscle tightening.
The head is in a natural alignment with the trunk and shoulders.
The mouth is open and the jaw is allowed to sag in the rear support phase.
The flight phase duration is 0.12 to 0.14 seconds.
The support phase duration is 0.08 to 0.10 seconds; up to 1,800 newtons (GRFs) in the support phase.
Eccentric hip flexion decelerates backward rotation of the thigh in the early flight phase.
Eccentric knee extension decelerates backward rotation of the leg/foot in the early flight phase.
Concentric hip flexion accelerates the thigh forward in the midflight phase.
Eccentric knee extension switches to eccentric knee flexion in the midflight phase.
Concentric hip extension rotates the thigh backward in preparation for foot contact in the late flight phase.
Eccentric knee flexion accelerates the leg backward, limiting knee extension; stops before foot strike (aided by concentric knee flexion to minimize braking at touchdown) in the late flight phase.
Continued concentric hip extension minimizes the braking effect of foot strike in the early support phase.
A brief concentric knee flexion followed by eccentric hip extension resists the tendency of hip/ankle extension to hyperextend the knee; absorbs landing shock in the early support phase.
Eccentric plantarflexion helps absorb shock and control forward rotation of the tibia over the ankle in the early support phase.
Eccentric hip flexion decelerates backward thigh rotation; rotates the trunk in preparation for forward takeoff in the late support phase.
Concentric knee extension propels the center of gravity forward in the late support phase.
Concentric plantarflexion aids in propulsion in the late support phase.
Optimal recovery mechanics are another important determinant of sprinting speed and efficiency because the athlete must properly position his or her leg for ground contact as it swings forward.
The role of explosive strength and the problems associated with overstriding—that is, counterproductive braking forces and increased ground contact times—then become apparent.
Table 17.1 itemizes common sprinting mistakes, as well as accompanying causes and corrections.
Mistakes are usually associated with fatigue, deficient coordinative or physical abilities (or both), improper coaching, or simple misunderstanding.
Some athletes have learned unsound mechanics.

In some cases, these may be due to incorrect coaching guidelines—for example, trying to stay low or lean forward at maximum velocity, minimizing foot lift during recovery in order to have quicker feet, or overstriding in an attempt to pull tAblE 17 .1 Sprinting Technique Errors, Causes, and Corrections

Some athletes' prior speed training experience consists of fatiguing sprint conditioning, which can reinforce unsound mechanics.
The bottom-line goal of sprinting is to achieve high stride frequency and optimal stride length, with a trajectory characterized by explosive horizontal push-off and minimal vertical impulse.
This involves reflexive ground contact and brief application of very high GRFs, with limited range of motion (ROM) at the ankle (by virtue of high stiffness regulation) during the support phase.

The following are some fundamental training objectives regarding performance enhancement and injury prevention (92, 139): Minimize braking forces at ground contact by maximizing the backward velocity of the leg and foot at touchdown and by planting the foot directly beneath the center of gravity.
Emphasize brief ground support times as a means of achieving rapid stride rate. As previously mentioned, this requires a high level of explosive strength.

Emphasize functional training of the hamstring muscle group with respect to its biarticular structure and dual role (simultaneous concentric hip extension and eccentric knee flexion) during late recovery. Eccentric knee flexor strength is the most important aspect limiting recovery of the leg as it swings forward.

Running speed is the interaction of stride frequency and stride length. The goal of sprinting is to achieve high stride frequency and optimal stride length, with explosive horizontal push-off and minimal vertical impulse.
Agility effectively comprises an athlete’s entire movement skill set, with linear running being one component of overall maneuverability.
In the international community, agility is often broadly defined as an athlete’s collective coordinative abilities (28, 49, 125).
These are the basic elements of technical skills used to perform motor tasks spanning the power spectrum from dynamic, gross activities to fine motor control tasks:
Adaptive ability— modification of action sequence upon observation or anticipation of novel or changing conditions and situations
Balance— static and dynamic equilibrium
Combinatory ability— coordination of body movements into a given action
Differentiation— accurate, economical adjustment of body movements and mechanics
Orientation— spatial and temporal control of body movements
Reactiveness— quick, well-directed response to stimuli
Rhythm— observation and implementation of dynamic motion pattern, timing, and variation
Coordinative abilities are believed to be most trainable in preadolescence, which is considered a critical or sensitive period for skill development (6, 9, 22, 28, 49, 72, 125, 132).

This window of opportunity begins to close during adolescence, during which the focus should progress from basic movement competencies and fitness qualities to specific skills and abilities—that is, from general to special preparation.
An agility needs analysis seems like a daunting challenge, considering the range of movement tasks involved in many sports.
Athletes must skillfully perform different modes of locomotion (e.g., running forward, backward, and laterally) and efficiently transition from one mode to another.
They must manage momentum while maneuvering and changing velocities.
They regularly support their bodies on a single limb, with the center of mass frequently outside its base of support.
External forces—especially gravity—confront them and constantly disturb their balance.
In many cases, athletes perform these tasks while manipulating objects and deploying complex strategies.

Current lines of evidence on athletes’ agility performance deal with cornering techniques (2, 18, 43, 44, 127), backward running performance (4, 21, 36, 123), the mechanics of certain maneuvers (e.g., crossover or sidestep cutting) with regard to mechanisms of injury (37, 56, 75, 89, 90, 110), and the specificity of certain agility skills in relation to
Agility is an expression of an athlete’s coordinative abilities, which are the basis of acceleration, maximum-velocity, and multidirectional skills.
Movement skills can be classified according to three basic motor behavior criteria: general versus special tasks, closed versus open tasks, and continuous versus discrete versus serial tasks.
General agility tasks target the development of one or more basic coordinative abilities, whereas special tasks unify them in a skill-specific manner.
The basis of this concept is the motor learning principle of practice specificity with respect to sensorimotor, processing, and contextual effects on acquisition, retention, and transfer.
Closed agility skills have programmed assignments and predictable or stable environments.
The athlete rather than the context determines where, when, and how to begin the action, with the objective of optimizing motor patterns and achieving consistent performances.
Examples of closed agility skills include preplanned tasks such as the pro agility drill and the T-test.
Open skills have nonprogrammed assignments and unpredictable or unstable environments.
The context changes during performance, and the training objective is to rapidly respond and adapt to new or unforeseen stimuli and situations.

Examples of open skills include unanticipated tasks with no predetermined structure (e.g., open-field dodging in team games) in which perceptual skills and feedback adjustments play more prominent roles than premovement planning.
Some tasks are performed in semipredictable circumstances (e.g., the assignment or environment is variable, but can be anticipated to some extent with experience and practice).
Continuous tasks have no identifiable start or finish, with activity beginning and ending arbitrarily.
They are usually performed at low or intermediate speeds due to their ongoing, cyclical nature.
From a motor control standpoint, premovement planning tends to be limited, and feedback and error detection and correction significantly influence performance.
In contrast, discrete tasks have a definite start and finish.
