The staff should make routine checks of the first aid kit to ensure that it contains all necessary supplies.
The primary goals of a strength and conditioning program are to lower the potential for injury and improve performance.
The person MOST responsible for determining the equipment needs for a strength and conditioning facility is the strength and conditioning director.
The assistant strength and conditioning professional should be certified.

The individuals who should provide approval when deciding which nonuniversity individuals and groups can use the university strength and conditioning facility are the team doctor and the strength and conditioning director.

Appropriate actions for the strength and conditioning director to take if a member of the crew team repeatedly wears sandals to the facility are to document the offense, explain the importance of wearing proper footwear, and dismiss the individual from the facility for one week.
The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings.
Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats.
A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.

The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings.
Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats.
A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
Anaerobic training primarily involves muscular adaptations, neural adaptations, and physiological adaptations to resistance training.
The primary metabolic demands of anaerobic training vary by sport.
The anatomical dead space is 125.
Anatomical planes are illustrated in Figure 73f.
Androgens are hormones that influence muscle growth and development.
Androstenediol/androstenedione is a type of androgen.
The angle of pennation refers to the angle between muscle fibers and the tendon.
Angular displacement is a measure of the change in angle.
Angular velocity is a measure of the rate of change in angular displacement.
Anorexia nervosa is an eating disorder characterized by a distorted body image and an intense fear of gaining weight.
An antagonist muscle opposes the action of an agonist muscle.
Anthropometry is the measurement of the human body.
Anti-estrogens are substances that block the effects of estrogen.
Antioxidants protect cells from damage caused by free radicals.
Anxiety is a state of mental and emotional distress.
Aortic valve is a valve in the heart that controls blood flow from the right ventricle to the pulmonary artery.
APMHR (age-predicted maximal heart rate) is a formula used to estimate the maximum heart rate for an individual.
The appendicular skeleton includes the limbs and their supporting structures.
AR (androgen receptor) is a protein that binds to androgens.
Arginine is an amino acid that plays a role in protein synthesis and nitric oxide production.
Arm swings are a type of movement used in various sports and exercises.
Arousal is a state of physiological and psychological activation.
Control techniques for arousal include relaxation, visualization, and breathing exercises.
Self-efficacy is the belief in one's ability to successfully perform a task.
Trait anxiety is a personality characteristic that reflects an individual's tendency to experience anxiety.
The arterial system is responsible for transporting blood throughout the body.
Arterioles are small arteries that regulate blood flow to capillaries.
Arteriovenous oxygen difference is the difference in oxygen content between arterial and venous blood.
Articular cartilage is a type of connective tissue that covers the ends of bones in joints.
Assistance exercises are exercises that support the primary movement of a compound exercise.
Association and dissociation are two different cognitive styles.
Assumption of risk is a legal concept that applies to activities with inherent risks.
Asthma is a chronic respiratory disease characterized by inflammation and narrowing of the airways.
ATC (Certified Athletic Trainer) is a healthcare professional who specializes in the prevention, diagnosis, treatment, and rehabilitation of sports-related injuries.
Athlete profile-based training is a personalized approach to training that considers an athlete's individual needs and goals.
Athletic trainer is a healthcare professional who provides medical care to athletes.
ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is the primary energy currency of cells.
ATPase (adenosine triphosphatase) is an enzyme that breaks down ATP to release energy.
Atrium is a chamber of the heart that receives blood from the body or lungs.
Attentional style refers to an individual's preferred way of focusing their attention.
Attention and skill in sport are closely related.
Autocrine secretion of hormones refers to the release of hormones that act on the same cell that produced them.
Autogenic inhibition and stretching is a technique that involves contracting a muscle group before stretching it.
Autogenic training is a relaxation technique that involves focusing on bodily sensations.
Axial skeleton includes the skull, vertebral column, and rib cage.
Back injuries can occur during various activities, including sports and lifting heavy objects.
Intra-abdominal pressure is the pressure exerted by the abdominal muscles on the spine.

Thomas R. Baechle, EdD; CSCS,*D; NSCA-CPT,*D, is chair of the exercise science and athletic training department at Creighton University in Omaha, Nebraska.
Thomas R. Baechle has earned numerous certifications and awards in his career covering more than 35 years as a fitness professional and academician.

Thomas R. Baechle has taught at various universities, held a variety of professional and civic offices, and volunteered for many national and international associations and organizations related to fitness and personal health.
Thomas R. Baechle is widely published and lectures frequently.
Thomas R. Baechle received the Outstanding Writing Achievement Award from Human Kinetics in 2007.
Thomas R. Baechle received the Merit for Excellence in Education and Development of Professional Standards from the International Fitness Institute in 2006.
Thomas R. Baechle received the Distinguished Faculty Service Award from Creighton University in 2002.
Thomas R. Baechle received the Lifetime Achievement Award from the NSCA in 1998.
Thomas R. Baechle makes his home with his wife, Susan, in Omaha.
Roger W. Earle, MA; CSCS,*D; NSCA-CPT,*D, earned his master’s degree from the University of Nebraska at Omaha in exercise science.
Roger W. Earle is the Director of Certification for the National Strength and Conditioning Association (NSCA).
Roger W. Earle is responsible for developing the Certified Strength and Conditioning Specialist (CSCS) and the NSCA-Certified Personal Trainer (NSCA-CPT) certification exams.
Roger W. Earle previously served as the head strength and conditioning coach and a faculty member of the exercise science and athletic training department at Creighton University in Omaha.
Roger W. Earle has over 20 years of experience as a personal fitness trainer for people of all ages and fitness levels.
Roger W. Earle frequently gives lectures at national and international conferences about designing personalized exercise and training programs.
Roger W. Earle coauthored the first and second editions of Fitness Weight Training.
Roger W. Earle coedited both the NSCA’s Essentials of Personal Training and the first and second editions of Essentials of Strength Training and Conditioning.
Roger W. Earle enjoys spending time with his wife, Tonya, and their four daughters at their home in Glendale, Arizona.
